medications.indication_rationale: First-line for common warts, applied nightly to the pared/filed lesion under tape occlusion; source prescribed salicylic acid 17% nightly under occlusion.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply nightly to lesions under occlusion after filing; continue for weeks (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Cryotherapy is an option for refractory warts; a GP/clinic procedure.
Picking/biting spreads warts by autoinoculation; sharing tools spreads to others — stop.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop picking/biting the warts; soak and file the dead skin before applying the acid; wash hands after treatment; don't share towels/nail clippers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Stops autoinoculation and lets the treatment work.

patient_education_points.point: These are common warts — a harmless skin virus, not cancer (the black dots are tiny blood vessels). They spread when you pick at them, so stopping picking is the most important step. Salicylic acid clears them over 4–12 weeks.
Soak and file the hard skin before applying the acid nightly, wash your hands after, and don't share towels or nail clippers. A GP can offer freezing (cryotherapy) for stubborn ones.

See a doctor if any lesion becomes ulcerated, bleeds on its own, grows quickly, becomes darkly/irregularly coloured, or doesn't clear — it should be examined (and biopsied if needed). Also review if the skin becomes hot, red and swollen (infection).

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response; cryotherapy for refractory; biopsy if atypical/non-resolving.

must_include_items: Recognise common warts; salicylic acid + filing/occlusion; cryotherapy option
errors_of_commission: Reassuring an atypical lesion without biopsy pathway
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires salicylic-acid/cryotherapy management, stop-picking advice, and a malignancy (atypical-lesion) safety-net.
